title: Design a scalable payment system
prompt: Design a payment service that processes card charges for a high-traffic e-commerce platform. Clients (web/mobile) call POST /payments to charge a customer. The system must guarantee: (1) the same logical payment is never charged twice even if the client retries on timeout, network blip, or duplicate submit; (2) money movement is recorded in an auditable, immutable way; (3) the charge to the external Payment Service Provider (PSP, e.g. Stripe) happens exactly-once from the customer's perspective even though the PSP call may fail ambiguously. Describe the API contract, data model, the idempotency mechanism, the ledger design, how you achieve exactly-once with an at-least-once world, and how the design scales horizontally. Then provide a reference implementation of the core charge handler.
hints: The client cannot know whether a timed-out request actually charged the card. Make the operation safe to repeat by having the client send a stable Idempotency-Key (a UUID it generates once per logical payment attempt and reuses on every retry).

Persist the idempotency key with the request fingerprint and the eventual response in its own table BEFORE you call the PSP. A unique constraint on the key turns concurrent duplicates into a single winner; losers read back the stored result.

True exactly-once delivery to an external system is impossible. Aim for effectively-once: make the PSP call idempotent too by passing your idempotency key as the PSP's idempotency key, and model the charge as a state machine (PENDING -> AUTHORIZED -> CAPTURED / FAILED) so a retry resumes instead of restarting.

Record money movement as an append-only double-entry ledger: every transaction inserts balanced debit and credit rows that sum to zero. Never UPDATE a balance in place; the balance is a projection (sum) over immutable entries.

Decouple the synchronous accept (write intent + idempotency row, return 202) from the asynchronous PSP capture via an outbox + worker, so a PSP outage does not take down your write path and you scale the capture workers independently.
explanation: The design rests on four pillars. (1) Idempotency at the edge: the client mints one Idempotency-Key per logical payment and reuses it on every retry. We claim the key with an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING guarded by a unique primary key, so even under concurrent duplicate submits exactly one request wins and proceeds; all others read back the stored response. We also store a request fingerprint to reject the dangerous case of the same key being reused with a different body. (2) Effectively-once to the PSP: true exactly-once across a network is provably impossible (the FLP/two-generals problem), so we settle for effectively-once by forwarding OUR idempotency key as the PSP's idempotency key. If we crash after the PSP charged but before we recorded it, the retry hits the PSP again but the PSP de-duplicates and returns the original charge. The payment is a state machine (PENDING -> CAPTURED/FAILED) so a resumed job advances rather than re-charges. (3) Immutable double-entry ledger: money movement is never an in-place UPDATE of a balance; each transaction appends balanced DEBIT/CREDIT rows that sum to zero, giving a fully auditable history and letting balances be recomputed as a projection. (4) Transactional outbox: the synchronous handler only writes the intent + outbox row in one ACID transaction and returns 202, decoupling the fast accept path from the slow/failure-prone PSP capture. A pool of workers drains the outbox using FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, which lets you scale capture horizontally without double-processing. This separation also means a PSP outage degrades to delayed capture, not failed writes. Scaling: the API tier is stateless behind a load balancer; idempotency_keys and payments are partitionable by customer; the ledger is append-only (write-friendly, shard by account); workers scale independently of the API.

title: Fix the transaction problem
hints: Spring's @Transactional is implemented with an AOP proxy. The transactional behavior lives on the proxy, not on the raw object instance.
When batchProcess() calls saveOrder(dto) it is calling this.saveOrder(...) directly on the target object, bypassing the proxy entirely. The advice that opens/commits the transaction never runs.
Self-invocation is the classic trap: an internal method call never goes through the proxy, so any @Transactional, @Cacheable, @Async, or @Retryable on the internal method is silently ignored.
Fix it by routing the call through the proxy: extract saveOrder into a separate Spring bean that batchProcess injects and calls, or inject a self-reference / use AopContext.currentProxy().

Because each order must roll back independently, use propagation REQUIRES_NEW (a fresh transaction per order) so one failure does not poison the others, and decide explicitly whether batchProcess itself should be transactional.
explanation: Root cause: @Transactional in Spring is woven in by an AOP proxy. At runtime the bean injected into other components is not your OrderService instance directly but a proxy (CGLIB subclass or JDK dynamic proxy) whose overridden methods open a transaction before delegating and commit/rollback after. The transactional advice only fires when a call enters the bean from the OUTSIDE through that proxy. In the broken code, batchProcess() calls saveOrder(dto), which the compiler resolves to this.saveOrder(dto) on the raw target object. That call never touches the proxy, so the transaction-management advice is skipped entirely: saveOrder() runs with whatever transaction context happened to already exist (often none), and the @Transactional annotation is effectively dead. This is the well-known self-invocation limitation, and it bites @Transactional, @Cacheable, @Async, @Retryable, and @Validated identically. Fix: make the call cross a proxy boundary. The cleanest approach is to extract saveOrder into a separate Spring bean (OrderProcessor) and inject it; calls to processor.saveOrder() go through that bean's proxy, so the advice runs. We use propagation = REQUIRES_NEW so every order executes in its own suspended-and-fresh transaction, guaranteeing each order commits or rolls back independently; a single bad order no longer affects the others, and batchProcess catches the exception to keep iterating. We also set rollbackFor = Exception.class to be explicit (by default Spring only rolls back on unchecked exceptions and Errors). The commented alternative shows the in-class escape hatch using AopContext.currentProxy() with exposeProxy=true, or injecting a self-reference, for cases where extracting a second bean is undesirable. Whichever you choose, decide deliberately whether batchProcess itself should be transactional; with REQUIRES_NEW children, an outer transaction is usually unnecessary and would defeat the independent-rollback requirement.

title: Optimize Angular rendering
prompt: An Angular 18 dashboard renders a list of 10,000 transactions and feels janky: scrolling stutters, typing in an unrelated search box freezes the page, and the Angular DevTools profiler shows the whole list re-rendering on every mouse move. The component uses default change detection, *ngFor without trackBy, and recomputes a getter in the template. Refactor the component for performance using OnPush change detection, trackBy (or the new @for track), signals to make state changes granular, and virtual scrolling so only visible rows are in the DOM. Provide the optimized component.
hints: Default change detection re-checks every binding in the component tree on every event (clicks, mouse moves, timers, XHR). Switch the component to ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush so it only re-checks when an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside it, or a signal/observable it reads emits.

*ngFor without identity tracking destroys and recreates every DOM node when the array reference changes. Add trackBy (template-driven) or use the new control-flow @for (item of items; track item.id) so Angular reuses DOM nodes for unchanged items.

Never call a method or getter in a template binding for derived data; it runs on every change-detection pass. Replace it with a computed() signal so the value is cached and only recalculates when its inputs change.
Holding 10,000 rows in the DOM is the real killer. Use Angular CDK's <cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport> with *cdkVirtualFor so only the ~20 visible rows exist in the DOM at any time.
Convert mutable arrays to signals and update them immutably (set/update with a new reference) so OnPush and computed() detect the change reliably.
explanation: Each fix targets a distinct cause of the jank. OnPush: with the default strategy Angular dirty-checks every binding of every component on every browser event, including mouse moves — that is why moving the mouse re-renders the list. ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush restricts checks to three triggers: an @Input reference change, an event originating inside the component, or a signal/observable the template reads. Because all state here is signals, OnPush + signals give surgical updates: only the bindings that actually depend on a changed signal are refreshed. Track identity: a plain *ngFor keyed by object identity tears down and rebuilds every <div> whenever the array reference changes, throwing away DOM and component state. trackById (or @for (... ; track tx.id) in the new control flow) lets Angular match old and new items by a stable key and reuse the existing DOM nodes, so only genuinely changed rows touch the DOM. No template getters: calling a method/getter such as getTotal() in an interpolation re-executes on every change-detection pass, an unbounded cost. Moving derived values into computed() signals memoizes them — totalLabel() recomputes only when filtered() changes, and filtered() only when all() or query() change. Virtual scrolling: even with the above, keeping 10,000 rows in the DOM is the dominant cost; layout and paint scale with node count. cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport with *cdkVirtualFor renders only the rows in the viewport (about a dozen plus a small buffer) and recycles them as you scroll, capping DOM size regardless of dataset length. Immutable updates: signals detect change by reference for objects/arrays, so we replace the array with all.set(rows) rather than mutating in place, ensuring OnPush and computed() reliably observe the update. Together these take the component from O(events x bindings x 10000) work down to O(visible rows), eliminating the scroll stutter and the unrelated-input freeze.

title: Build a typeahead search without hammering the API
prompt: A search box fires an HTTP request on every keystroke, flooding the backend and producing out-of-order results where an older response overwrites a newer one. Implement a robust RxJS typeahead pipeline that debounces input, skips duplicate consecutive terms, ignores empty queries, cancels stale in-flight requests, and surfaces errors without killing the stream.
hints: Start from the input as an Observable<string> (valueChanges of a FormControl).
Use debounceTime to wait for a pause in typing, and distinctUntilChanged so retyping the same term does not re-fetch.
The out-of-order bug is solved by switchMap: it cancels the previous inner HTTP subscription when a new term arrives, so only the latest response wins.
Guard against empty terms with filter, and use catchError INSIDE the inner observable so a single failed request returns a fallback instead of completing the outer stream.
explanation: Each operator removes a specific failure mode. debounceTime(300) collapses a burst of keystrokes into a single emission once the user pauses, cutting request volume dramatically. distinctUntilChanged() suppresses redundant fetches when the term ends up identical (e.g. typing then deleting back). filter(length >= 2) drops empty and too-short queries that would return useless or huge result sets. The crucial operator is switchMap: unlike mergeMap, it unsubscribes from the previous inner HTTP observable the moment a new term arrives, cancelling the stale request so a slow earlier response can never overwrite a fresher one — that is precisely the out-of-order race the prompt describes. Finally, catchError is placed INSIDE the switchMap so an error from one request is contained to that request and mapped to a safe fallback (of([])); if catchError were on the outer pipe instead, the first error would terminate the entire stream and the search box would stop working until re-initialized.

title: Model API state with a discriminated union
prompt: A component models async state with three loose booleans: isLoading, hasError, and a data field, plus an errorMessage string. This allows impossible states like isLoading=true while data is also set, and forces defensive null checks everywhere. Redesign the state as a TypeScript discriminated union that makes illegal states unrepresentable and lets the compiler enforce exhaustive handling.
hints: List the states that can actually occur: idle, loading, success (carries data), error (carries a message). Only one is true at a time.
Give each variant a shared literal discriminant property (e.g. status: 'success') and only the fields valid for that variant.
When you switch on the discriminant, TypeScript narrows the type so data/error are only accessible in the right branch.
Add a default branch with a 'never' assertion so adding a new variant later forces a compile error until you handle it.
explanation: The boolean-soup design admits 2^3 = 8 flag combinations, most of which are nonsensical (loading and error simultaneously, success with no data, etc.), and every consumer must defensively check for those impossible cases. A discriminated union encodes the truth that the state is exactly one of a small set of mutually exclusive variants. The shared literal property status is the discriminant: when you switch on it, TypeScript narrows the union inside each case so that, for example, data exists only in the success branch and message only in the error branch — accessing data in the error branch is a compile error, eliminating an entire class of bugs and null checks. The default branch calls assertNever(state), whose parameter is typed never. As long as every variant is handled, state is narrowed to never by the time control reaches default and the code compiles. The moment someone adds a new variant (say { status: 'cancelled' }) without a matching case, state is no longer never there, the call fails to type-check, and the compiler forces you to handle the new state. This is making illegal states unrepresentable plus compiler-enforced exhaustiveness.

title: Make a REST API safe to retry
prompt: A mobile client retries failed requests automatically, but your POST /transfers endpoint creates a duplicate transfer every time a response is lost on the network. Design and implement an idempotent transfer endpoint so that retries with the same client-supplied key are processed at most once, returning the original result. Discuss which HTTP methods are naturally idempotent and why POST is not.
hints: GET, PUT, and DELETE are defined as idempotent: repeating them has the same effect as doing them once. POST is not, because each call is meant to create a new resource.
Have the client send an Idempotency-Key header (a UUID it generates once and reuses on retries).
Persist the key with the computed response in a store with a unique constraint, so concurrent duplicates collide and only one performs the side effect.
On a repeated key, short-circuit and return the stored response instead of re-executing the transfer; expire keys after a TTL (e.g. 24h).
explanation: HTTP defines GET, HEAD, PUT, and DELETE as idempotent: issuing the same request N times leaves the server in the same state as issuing it once (PUT replaces a resource with a fixed representation; DELETE removes it; GET has no side effect). POST is explicitly non-idempotent because its contract is 'create a new subordinate resource' — every call is supposed to make something new, which is exactly why a retried POST duplicates a transfer. To make a POST safe to retry we add application-level idempotency. The client generates an Idempotency-Key once per logical operation and reuses it across retries. The server atomically claims the key (Redis SET NX with TTL, or an INSERT against a unique column) so that under concurrent duplicate requests exactly one wins the claim and performs the side effect; the losers see the key already taken. The winner executes the transfer once, stores its response under the key, and returns it. Any later retry with the same key finds the stored response and returns it verbatim — at-most-once execution with the original result preserved. While the original is still running, duplicates get a 409 telling them to retry the read. Passing the key downstream (reference: key) propagates idempotency to the bank so the whole chain is safe. Keys expire after a TTL to bound storage; the window should comfortably exceed the client's maximum retry horizon.

title: Eliminate the N+1 query problem
prompt: An endpoint that lists 100 authors and their books issues 101 SQL queries and is slow under load. The JPA code loads authors, then iterates and touches each author's lazily-loaded books collection. Identify the N+1 problem and fix it so the data loads in a constant number of queries. Show both a JPQL fetch-join fix and a note on entity-graph and batch-size alternatives.
hints: Count the queries: 1 to fetch the authors, then 1 more per author when its lazy 'books' collection is first accessed = 1 + N.
A JOIN FETCH in JPQL loads the association in the same query, collapsing N+1 into 1 (or 2 with pagination).
Beware: JOIN FETCH a collection multiplies rows (one per book); use DISTINCT and remember it breaks SQL-level pagination on the collection.
Alternatives: @EntityGraph on the repository method, or hibernate.default_batch_fetch_size to turn N selects into ceil(N/batch) IN-clause selects.
explanation: The N+1 problem occurs when an ORM issues one query to fetch a list of parent rows and then one additional query per parent to lazily load an association. Here findAll() runs 1 query for 100 authors, and the first access to each author's books triggers a separate SELECT, for 1 + 100 = 101 round trips — each with network and planning overhead that dominates under load. The primary fix is a JPQL JOIN FETCH, which instructs Hibernate to load the authors and their books in a single SQL join, eliminating the per-author queries. Because joining a to-many collection produces one row per book, the same author appears multiple times in the result set; SELECT DISTINCT de-duplicates the managed entities. A caveat: fetch-joining a collection makes database-level LIMIT/OFFSET pagination incorrect (the limit applies to the joined rows, not authors), so for paginated parents prefer alternatives. @EntityGraph achieves the same eager load declaratively while letting you keep Spring Data derived queries. When you must paginate, hibernate.default_batch_fetch_size is the best tool: it leaves authors lazily loaded but, when their collections are accessed, batches the missing ones into IN-clause selects, turning N queries into ceil(N / batchSize) — e.g. 100 authors with batch size 50 becomes 2 queries instead of 100, without breaking pagination. The right choice depends on whether you paginate and how large the collections are.

title: Process Kafka messages without duplicates or loss
prompt: A Kafka consumer that updates a database occasionally double-applies a message (after a rebalance) and occasionally loses one (after a crash). The current code auto-commits offsets and processes messages in a fire-and-forget loop. Redesign the consume-process-commit flow to achieve effectively-once processing against the database, explaining the ordering of side effect vs offset commit and the role of idempotency.
hints: Auto-commit advances the offset on a timer independent of whether your processing succeeded — that is how you both lose messages (committed before processing) and reprocess them.
Turn off auto-commit and commit the offset only AFTER the side effect is durable. This gives at-least-once: never lose, but may reprocess on crash/rebalance.
At-least-once + idempotent writes = effectively-once. Make the DB write idempotent using the message key or an UPSERT / dedup table keyed by (topic, partition, offset) or a business id.

For true transactional exactly-once across DB and Kafka, either write offsets into the same DB transaction as the side effect (transactional outbox/inbox), or use Kafka transactions for Kafka-to-Kafka pipelines.
explanation: Kafka delivery guarantees are determined by WHEN you commit the offset relative to your side effect. Auto-commit is the worst of both worlds: a background thread advances offsets on a timer regardless of processing outcome. If the commit lands before your DB write and you then crash, the message is skipped forever (loss); if you crash after writing but before the timer commit, or a rebalance reassigns the partition, the message is reprocessed (duplicate). The first fix is to disable auto-commit and call commitSync only AFTER the side effects for that partition are durably written. This guarantees at-least-once: you never lose a message, but a crash between the write and the commit still causes reprocessing on restart. To make at-least-once safe, the processing must be idempotent. Here we dedup on the natural unique identity of a record — (topic, partition, offset) — using INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING in the same transaction as the business effect; a reprocessed record finds its offset row already present, the insert is a no-op, and the business effect is skipped. Combined, at-least-once delivery plus idempotent application yields effectively-once results: every message is applied exactly one time to the database state even though Kafka may deliver it more than once. We commit per partition after its records are durable, and we set isolation.level=read_committed so we never consume aborted transactional messages. For a stronger transactional guarantee you can store the offset inside the same database transaction as the side effect (the inbox pattern), which removes the tiny window between the DB commit and the Kafka commit entirely; for pure Kafka-to-Kafka pipelines, Kafka's transactional producer with read-process-write gives end-to-end exactly-once.

title: Prevent a cache stampede
prompt: A hot key in Redis expires and thousands of concurrent requests all miss the cache simultaneously, slam the database to recompute the same value, and the site falls over (a cache stampede / thundering herd). Design a caching layer that lets only one request recompute an expired value while others serve a slightly stale value or wait, and explain mitigations like locking, early recomputation, and request coalescing.
hints: The core problem is that an expiry is a single instant where every reader simultaneously becomes a writer. You want at most one recomputation per key.
Use a short-lived distributed lock (SET lock NX PX) so only the lock winner recomputes; others either wait-and-retry the cache or serve the stale value.

Store the value with a logical expiry that is EARLIER than the physical TTL, so one request refreshes the entry probabilistically before it actually disappears (early/probabilistic recomputation, the XFetch technique).
Within a single process, coalesce concurrent misses for the same key into one in-flight promise so you don't even hit Redis N times.
explanation: A stampede happens because a cache expiry is a synchronization point: at the instant a hot key disappears, every concurrent reader turns into a database writer recomputing the identical value, and that burst overwhelms the backend. Three complementary mitigations tame it. (1) Distributed locking: only the request that wins SET lock NX PX recomputes; everyone else briefly waits and then reads the value the winner just wrote, so the database sees one recomputation instead of thousands. The lock has a PX timeout so a crashed holder cannot deadlock the key. (2) Probabilistic early recomputation (the XFetch / early-expiration technique): we store a logical expiry earlier than the physical TTL and have callers refresh with a small random probability as that logical expiry approaches. This spreads refreshes out in time and ensures the key is renewed BEFORE it physically vanishes, so most readers never even encounter a miss — eliminating the simultaneous-expiry instant that causes the herd. While refreshing, we keep serving the still-valid stale value, so latency stays flat. (3) In-process request coalescing: an inFlight promise map collapses many concurrent callers for the same key within a single process into one shared computation, so you don't multiply load even before reaching Redis. Together these guarantee at most one recomputation per key per refresh window, bound database load, and avoid both the latency cliff and the outage that an unprotected expiry causes. Optional hardening includes serving stale-while-revalidate on errors and jittering TTLs so unrelated keys don't expire in lockstep.

title: Resolve a database deadlock between two transfers
prompt: Two concurrent money-transfer transactions sometimes fail with a deadlock: transfer A moves money from account 1 to account 2 while transfer B moves money from account 2 to account 1. Each locks the first account, then blocks trying to lock the second, and the database kills one as a deadlock victim. Explain the deadlock cycle and rewrite the transfer logic to prevent it, plus describe how to handle the unavoidable cases.
hints: Draw the wait-for graph: A holds lock(1) and waits for lock(2); B holds lock(2) and waits for lock(1). That cycle IS the deadlock.
Deadlocks need a circular wait. Break the cycle by always acquiring row locks in a consistent global order — e.g. ascending account id — regardless of transfer direction.
Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the accounts in that fixed order to take the locks deterministically before mutating balances.
You still can't prevent every deadlock (e.g. with index/gap locks), so wrap the transaction in a bounded retry on the deadlock error code and keep transactions short.
explanation: A deadlock requires four conditions, and the breakable one here is circular wait. In the buggy version each transaction locks its source account first, then its destination. Transfer A (1->2) acquires lock(1) and requests lock(2); transfer B (2->1) acquires lock(2) and requests lock(1). The wait-for graph now has a cycle A->B->A: neither can proceed, so the database detects it and aborts one as the deadlock victim. The fix removes the possibility of a cycle by imposing a total order on lock acquisition: every transaction locks the involved accounts in ascending id order, no matter which direction money flows. With a consistent order, two transactions touching the same pair will both try to lock the lower id first; one wins and the other simply waits for it and then proceeds — a linear wait, never a cycle. We take the locks explicitly with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE in that fixed order before mutating any balance, so the lock acquisition is deterministic rather than incidental to the UPDATE statements. Because index range locks, gap locks, or other transactions can still occasionally produce deadlocks that ordering alone cannot prevent, production code wraps the transaction in a short, bounded retry that catches the deadlock error (e.g. PostgreSQL SQLSTATE 40P01 / Spring's DeadlockLoserDataAccessException) with a little backoff, and keeps transactions small and fast to shrink the window in which contention can occur.

title: Design stateless authentication with JWTs and refresh tokens
prompt: An API uses long-lived JWT access tokens stored in localStorage and has no way to revoke them, so a stolen token works until it expires days later. Redesign the auth flow: short-lived access tokens, rotating refresh tokens, server-side revocation, and safe storage. Explain the tradeoffs of stateless JWTs versus server sessions and how to mitigate the revocation gap.
hints: JWTs are self-validating, so the server can't 'forget' one. Limit blast radius by making access tokens short-lived (5–15 min).
Pair the access token with a long-lived refresh token that is opaque and stored server-side, so it CAN be revoked.
Rotate the refresh token on every use and detect reuse of an already-rotated token as a theft signal — revoke the whole family.
Store the refresh token in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie (not localStorage) to resist XSS; keep the access token in memory.
explanation: Stateless JWTs scale well because any node can verify a token from its signature without a session lookup, but that same property is their weakness: there is no server state to invalidate, so you cannot truly revoke an issued access token before it expires. The design keeps the JWT benefit while bounding the damage. Access tokens are short-lived (about 10 minutes) and carry only identity and roles; if one is stolen it is useless almost immediately. Authority to obtain new access tokens lives in a refresh token, which is the opposite kind of credential: long-lived, opaque (not a JWT), and stored server-side as a hash so it CAN be revoked. On every refresh we rotate: the presented token is marked rotated and a brand-new refresh token is issued within the same family. This enables theft detection — if an attacker steals a refresh token and uses it, then the legitimate client later presents the same (now-rotated) token, the server sees a reuse of an already-rotated token and revokes the entire family, logging everyone out and cutting off the attacker. Storage matters: the refresh token goes in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=strict cookie scoped to the refresh path so JavaScript (and thus XSS) cannot read it, while the access token is held only in memory so it is not persisted where XSS or other tabs can grab it. Logout and admin revocation simply revoke the family server-side. The residual revocation gap — the few minutes an access token stays valid after a logout — is the deliberate tradeoff for statelessness; if you need instant revocation you add a short-TTL denylist of token IDs (jti) checked on each request, which reintroduces a small amount of state but only for revoked tokens.

title: Coordinate a distributed transaction with the Saga pattern
prompt: An order workflow spans three microservices — Order, Payment, and Inventory — each with its own database. You cannot use a two-phase commit across them. Place an order so that it either fully completes (order created, payment captured, stock reserved) or fully unwinds, with no money taken when stock is unavailable. Design this as a Saga, contrast orchestration vs choreography, and address compensation, idempotency, and partial failure.
hints: There is no global ACID transaction across separate databases. A Saga replaces it with a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action that semantically undoes it.

Choreography: services react to each other's events with no central coordinator (simple but the flow is implicit and hard to trace). Orchestration: a central coordinator tells each service what to do and drives compensation (explicit, easier to reason about).
Every step and every compensation must be idempotent, because the orchestrator will retry on timeouts and may re-deliver commands.
On a failure at step N, run the compensations for steps N-1..1 in reverse order (refund payment, release stock). Compensations must always eventually succeed, so make them retryable and commutative.
Persist saga state durably so a crashed orchestrator resumes mid-flow rather than leaving a dangling half-completed order.
explanation: Because each microservice owns a separate database, a two-phase commit is unavailable (and undesirable — it couples services and blocks under failure). The Saga pattern replaces one distributed ACID transaction with a sequence of local ACID transactions, each paired with a compensating transaction that semantically undoes its effect (cancel the order, release the reservation, refund the payment). The system reaches eventual consistency: either all forward steps commit, or all completed steps are compensated, so money is never captured when stock cannot be reserved. There are two coordination styles. In choreography, each service emits events and the next service reacts; there is no central brain, which is lightweight but makes the end-to-end flow implicit, hard to trace, and prone to cyclic event tangles as steps grow. In orchestration (shown here), a central orchestrator holds the workflow definition, issues commands, and explicitly drives compensation on failure; it costs a coordinator component but yields a single place to reason about the flow, observe progress, and resume after crashes. Three properties make it correct under partial failure. Idempotency: every forward step and every compensation carries the sagaId and must tolerate redelivery, because the orchestrator retries on timeouts and cannot always tell whether a previous attempt succeeded. Reverse-order compensation: when step N fails, we compensate steps N-1..1 in reverse, undoing the most recent effects first; compensations must be retried until they eventually succeed (a refund cannot be allowed to silently fail) and should be commutative/safe to repeat. Durable saga state: we persist the saga's status and current step before each transition, so a crashed orchestrator reloads in-flight sagas on restart and resumes either forward or into compensation, never leaving a half-completed order stranded. Note that sagas relax isolation — intermediate states are visible — so you use semantic locks or status fields (e.g. order PENDING until confirmed) to prevent acting on uncommitted intermediate state.

title: Split reads from writes with CQRS
prompt: A single normalized order model serves both a high-volume analytics dashboard and the transactional order-placement path. Complex read joins are slowing down writes, and write-optimized normalization makes the dashboard queries painful. Propose a CQRS design that separates the command (write) model from the query (read) model, explain how the read side stays in sync, and lay out the consistency tradeoffs and when CQRS is NOT worth it.
hints: CQRS = Command Query Responsibility Segregation: use one model optimized for writes and a SEPARATE model optimized for reads, instead of forcing one schema to do both jobs.
Commands change state and return no data; queries return data and never change state. Keep them in different code paths and possibly different stores.
Sync the read model by publishing domain events from the write side and projecting them into denormalized read views (often eventually consistent).
Be explicit that the read model lags the write model (eventual consistency); decide where that's acceptable and where you need read-your-writes.
CQRS adds real complexity — only adopt it where read and write workloads genuinely diverge; for simple CRUD it is over-engineering.
explanation: CQRS separates the responsibility of changing state (commands) from the responsibility of reading state (queries), and lets each side use a model shaped for its own job. The write model stays normalized and behavior-rich: it enforces invariants on the Order aggregate and is optimized for correct, contention-free mutation. The read model is denormalized and query-shaped: dashboard rows are pre-joined and pre-aggregated so a query is a single fast lookup with no joins, and analytics rollups (e.g. daily revenue) are maintained incrementally. This removes the original conflict — heavy read joins no longer compete with the write path, and the write schema no longer has to compromise for reporting. Synchronization is event-driven: after a command commits, the write side publishes a domain event (OrderPlaced); a projector consumes it and updates the read views. Because that propagation is asynchronous, the read side is eventually consistent — it lags the write side by the projection delay. You must decide where that lag is acceptable (analytics dashboards: clearly yes) and where you need read-your-writes semantics (e.g. showing a user the order they just placed), which you handle by reading from the write model for that specific case or by waiting for the projection. The tradeoffs are real: CQRS introduces two models, an event pipeline, projection code, and the operational burden of eventual consistency and reprocessing/rebuilding read models. It pays off precisely when read and write workloads diverge sharply in shape, scale, or scaling needs — exactly this scenario. For ordinary CRUD where a single model serves both fine, CQRS is over-engineering and you should not adopt it. CQRS also pairs naturally with, but does not require, event sourcing.

title: Implement a distributed rate limiter
prompt: A public API needs to limit each client to 100 requests per minute, enforced consistently across a fleet of stateless API servers behind a load balancer. A naive per-server in-memory counter lets a client get 100 x N requests by hitting different servers. Design a distributed rate limiter, choose an algorithm (fixed window vs sliding window vs token bucket), and implement it atomically so concurrent requests can't over-admit.
hints: Per-server counters don't work because the limit is global; the counter must live in shared state (e.g. Redis) that all servers consult.
Fixed windows allow a 2x burst at the window boundary; token bucket and sliding-window-log/counter smooth this out. Token bucket also naturally allows controlled bursts.
The check-and-decrement must be atomic across concurrent requests, or two requests can both read 1 token left and both proceed. Use a Redis Lua script (single-threaded, atomic) or atomic INCR.
Store per-client state (tokens + last refill timestamp) keyed by client id, and refill lazily based on elapsed time at request time rather than on a background timer.
solutionCode:
-- Token-bucket rate limiter as an atomic Redis Lua script.
-- KEYS[1] = bucket key (per client). ARGV: capacity, refillPerSec, now(ms), requested
local key        = KEYS[1]
local capacity   = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local refillRate = tonumber(ARGV[2])   -- tokens per second
local now        = tonumber(ARGV[3])   -- current time in ms
local requested  = tonumber(ARGV[4])

local state  = redis.call('HMGET', key, 'tokens', 'ts')
local tokens = tonumber(state[1])
local ts     = tonumber(state[2])
if tokens == nil then tokens = capacity; ts = now end

-- Lazy refill based on elapsed time (no background timer needed).
local elapsed = math.max(0, now - ts) / 1000.0
tokens = math.min(capacity, tokens + elapsed * refillRate)

local allowed = 0
if tokens >= requested then
  tokens = tokens - requested
  allowed = 1
end

redis.call('HMSET', key, 'tokens', tokens, 'ts', now)
redis.call('PEXPIRE', key, math.ceil(capacity / refillRate * 1000) * 2)
return { allowed, math.floor(tokens) }

-- Caller (Node): 100 req / 60s => capacity 100, refill 100/60 tokens/sec.
-- const [allowed, remaining] = await redis.eval(SCRIPT, 1, `rl:${clientId}`,
--     100, 100/60, Date.now(), 1);
-- if (!allowed) return res.status(429).set('Retry-After','1').end();
explanation: The limit is global (100/min per client across the whole fleet), so the counter cannot live in any single server's memory — a client could multiply its quota by spreading requests across servers. The state must reside in a store all servers share, here Redis. Algorithm choice matters: a fixed-window counter is simplest but allows a burst of up to 2x the limit straddling a window boundary (100 requests in the last second of one window plus 100 in the first second of the next). A sliding-window log is precise but stores a timestamp per request (memory-heavy); a sliding-window counter approximates it cheaply. Token bucket, used here, refills tokens at a steady rate up to a capacity, which both enforces the average rate and permits controlled short bursts up to the bucket size — a good fit for APIs. Crucially the operation must be atomic: if two concurrent requests each read 'tokens = 1' before either decrements, both would be admitted and the limit is breached. We get atomicity by running the whole read-refill-check-write sequence as a Redis Lua script; Redis executes scripts single-threaded and atomically, so no interleaving is possible and over-admission cannot occur. Refill is lazy: instead of a background timer topping up every bucket, we compute tokens added since the last access from elapsed time at request time, which scales to millions of keys with zero background work. We set a PEXPIRE so idle clients' buckets self-evict. The script returns whether the request is allowed and the remaining tokens, letting the caller emit 429 with a Retry-After header when the bucket is empty.

title: Let browsers upload large files directly to S3
prompt: Users upload large videos through your API server, which proxies the bytes to S3. This saturates your servers' bandwidth and memory and times out on big files. Redesign so the browser uploads directly to S3 without the files passing through your servers, while keeping your bucket private and the upload authorized and constrained. Implement the presigned-URL flow and explain the security controls.
hints: Don't proxy bytes through your app at all. Your server's job is only to authorize and hand out a short-lived presigned URL/POST that grants permission to PUT a single object.
Keep the bucket private (no public write). The presigned credential is derived from your server's IAM permissions but scoped to one key and a short expiry.
Use a presigned POST (or PUT) with conditions constraining content-length-range and content-type so a client can't upload arbitrary huge or wrong-type files.
Generate a server-controlled object key (don't trust client filenames), and confirm completion via an S3 event or a follow-up callback before marking the upload usable.
explanation: Proxying upload bytes through your application forces every gigabyte to traverse your servers, consuming their bandwidth, memory, and request time and causing timeouts on large files — your servers become a pointless middleman for data that ultimately belongs in S3. The presigned-URL pattern removes them from the data path. Your server's role shrinks to authorization: it authenticates the user, decides what they may upload, and uses its own IAM credentials to generate a short-lived presigned POST that grants permission to write exactly one object. The bytes then flow browser-to-S3 directly; your servers never see them. Security is preserved by tightly scoping the grant. The bucket stays private with no public write access — the presigned credential is the only thing that authorizes this specific upload, and it expires in seconds. Conditions lock it down: content-length-range caps the object size so a client cannot upload a multi-terabyte file or a zero-byte placeholder, and an eq condition on Content-Type prevents uploading a disallowed type. The object key is generated server-side from the user's id plus a random UUID, so a malicious client cannot choose a path to overwrite someone else's object or escape its prefix. Finally, because the upload bypasses your server you cannot assume success synchronously: you record the upload as PENDING and only mark it READY when S3 confirms the object was created, via an S3 event notification (EventBridge/SQS) or a verified callback. This yields a scalable, secure flow where the API does authorization and bookkeeping while S3 handles the heavy data transfer.

title: Tame AWS Lambda cold starts
prompt: A latency-sensitive Lambda behind API Gateway shows p99 spikes of 3+ seconds whenever traffic ramps, traced to cold starts. The function is a heavy JVM service that opens a database connection in the handler and initializes large SDK clients per invocation. Explain what a cold start is and its phases, then list and implement concrete mitigations, distinguishing what helps init latency vs steady-state.
hints: A cold start happens when no warm execution environment exists: AWS must download your code, start the runtime, and run your init code before the handler. Subsequent invocations on that environment are warm.
Move expensive one-time work (SDK clients, DB connection pools, config) OUT of the handler into the module/static init scope so it runs once per environment, not per request.
Cut init cost: smaller deployment package, fewer/lighter dependencies, and for JVM consider AWS SnapStart or a GraalVM native image; lighter runtimes (Node/Python/Go) cold-start faster than JVM.
For predictable low latency use Provisioned Concurrency (pre-initialized environments) and/or right-size memory (more memory = more CPU = faster init and execution).
Reuse connections across invocations and keep them in init scope; never open a fresh DB connection inside the handler.
explanation: A cold start is the latency incurred when an invocation arrives and there is no warm execution environment ready, so AWS must (1) provision an environment and download your deployment package, (2) start the language runtime, and (3) run your initialization code (static blocks, constructors, top-level module code) before your handler executes. Only after that does the handler run. Once an environment exists it stays warm for a while and serves subsequent invocations without these phases — those are warm starts. Spikes during traffic ramps occur because new concurrency forces new environments to cold-start in parallel. The single most impactful code change is to move all expensive one-time work out of the handler and into init scope: SDK clients, the database connection pool, and configuration are created once per environment (during the cold start) and reused across every warm invocation, instead of being rebuilt on each request. Opening a DB connection inside the handler is the classic anti-pattern shown in the prompt; placing the pool in static init fixes both the per-request cost and connection churn. To reduce the cold-start cost itself you shrink and lighten the package (fewer/smaller dependencies, a lighter HTTP client), and for the JVM specifically — which has the heaviest init of the common runtimes — you use AWS SnapStart (snapshots a pre-initialized environment and restores it in a fraction of the time) or compile a GraalVM native image for millisecond init; lighter runtimes like Node, Python, or Go avoid much of this. Raising MemorySize increases allocated vCPU, speeding up both init and execution. For workloads that cannot tolerate any cold start, Provisioned Concurrency keeps a pool of environments fully initialized and ready, eliminating cold starts for that capacity at a cost. The distinction matters: SnapStart, smaller packages, native images, and more memory attack INIT latency, while Provisioned Concurrency and warming attack the OCCURRENCE of cold starts, and connection reuse in init scope improves STEADY-STATE per-request latency.

title: Lock down an over-permissioned IAM role
prompt: An application role was shipped with a policy granting Action: '*' on Resource: '*' because 'it was easier during development.' Security flagged it. Rewrite it to least privilege for a service that reads from one S3 prefix, writes to one DynamoDB table, and reads one Secrets Manager secret. Explain least privilege, the risks of wildcards, and supporting controls like permission boundaries and conditions.
hints: Least privilege means granting only the specific actions on the specific resources the workload actually needs — nothing more.
Action:'*' Resource:'*' means the role can do ANYTHING to EVERYTHING; a single leaked credential becomes account takeover. Enumerate the real API calls instead.
Scope each statement to exact ARNs (the one bucket prefix, the one table, the one secret), not '*'.
Add Condition keys (e.g. require TLS via aws:SecureTransport, restrict source VPC) and use a permission boundary to cap what the role can ever be granted.
explanation: Least privilege is the principle that an identity should be granted only the exact permissions its workload requires to function — the minimal set of actions on the minimal set of resources — and nothing else. The shipped policy violates it maximally: Action '*' on Resource '*' lets the role perform every API call in the account against every resource, so if that credential is ever exposed (leaked env var, SSRF, compromised dependency) the attacker effectively owns the whole account — they can read every bucket, delete databases, create new admin users, and exfiltrate everything. Wildcards also defeat auditing and blast-radius reasoning. The rewrite enumerates only the operations the service actually performs and scopes each to precise ARNs: s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket limited to one bucket and the incoming/ prefix (note ListBucket targets the bucket ARN while GetObject targets the object ARN, and an s3:prefix condition constrains listing); three specific DynamoDB item actions on exactly the Orders table ARN; and secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on just the one secret ARN (the trailing -* accommodates Secrets Manager's random suffix). Conditions add defense in depth: aws:SecureTransport:true refuses any non-TLS request, and you can further constrain by source VPC endpoint or IP. Supporting controls complete the picture: a permission boundary attached to the role caps the maximum permissions it can EVER be granted even if someone later attaches a broader policy, which is how platform teams safely delegate role creation; and tools like IAM Access Analyzer can generate a tightened policy from observed CloudTrail activity so you grant exactly what the workload used. The net effect is that a compromise of this role exposes only one prefix, one table, and one secret instead of the entire account.

title: Ship a release with zero downtime
prompt: Deployments currently kill all old pods and start new ones at once, causing a brief outage and dropped in-flight requests on every release. Configure a Kubernetes Deployment for zero-downtime rolling updates, including readiness/liveness probes and graceful shutdown, and explain how rolling vs blue-green vs canary differ and when to use each.
hints: The outage comes from terminating old pods before new ones are ready and from sending traffic to pods that aren't actually serving yet.
A RollingUpdate with maxUnavailable: 0 keeps full capacity during the rollout; new pods must pass a readinessProbe before they receive traffic.

Graceful shutdown: handle SIGTERM, stop accepting new requests, finish in-flight ones, and set terminationGracePeriodSeconds plus a preStop hook so the pod leaves the Service endpoints before it dies.

Rolling = gradual in-place replacement; blue-green = two full environments with an instant switch and easy rollback; canary = route a small % of traffic to the new version first to validate before full rollout.
solutionCode:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: orders-api
spec:
  replicas: 4
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0     # never drop below desired capacity
      maxSurge: 1           # add one extra pod at a time
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: orders-api }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: orders-api }
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30   # time for in-flight requests to drain
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: registry/orders-api:1.4.0
          ports: [{ containerPort: 8080 }]
          # Only receives traffic once this passes -> no requests to unready pods.
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /health/ready, port: 8080 }
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 3
          # Restarts the pod if the process wedges (separate concern from readiness).
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /health/live, port: 8080 }
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 10
          lifecycle:
            preStop:
              # Pause so the pod is removed from Service endpoints BEFORE the app exits,
              # preventing the load balancer from sending it new requests mid-shutdown.
              exec: { command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 10"] }
          resources:
            requests: { cpu: "250m", memory: "256Mi" }
            limits:   { cpu: "500m", memory: "512Mi" }
# App must trap SIGTERM: stop accepting new connections, finish in-flight requests, then exit.
explanation: The outage has two root causes: old pods are removed before replacements are ready (a capacity gap), and traffic is routed to new pods before they can actually serve (early requests fail). The Deployment config closes both. strategy RollingUpdate with maxUnavailable: 0 guarantees the rollout never drops below the desired replica count — Kubernetes brings up a new pod (maxSurge: 1) and waits for it before retiring an old one, so full capacity is maintained throughout. The readinessProbe is what prevents serving traffic to a not-yet-ready pod: a pod is only added to the Service's endpoints once its readiness check passes, so requests are never sent to a pod still warming up, connecting to its database, or loading caches. The livenessProbe is a distinct concern — it restarts a pod whose process has wedged — and must not be conflated with readiness. Graceful shutdown handles in-flight requests during termination: when a pod is told to stop, Kubernetes sends SIGTERM and starts the terminationGracePeriodSeconds clock; the application must trap SIGTERM, stop accepting new connections, and finish the requests it is already serving before exiting. The preStop sleep covers a subtle race: endpoint removal and SIGTERM happen concurrently, so the brief sleep lets the load balancer observe the pod leaving the Service endpoints before the app actually shuts down, ensuring no new request is routed to a dying pod. Strategy choice: a rolling update gradually replaces pods in place — cheap and the default, but old and new versions run simultaneously so they must be compatible. Blue-green stands up a complete second environment on the new version and flips traffic over at once, giving an instant cutover and trivial rollback (flip back) at the cost of double resources. Canary routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version first, watches error and latency metrics, and only proceeds to full rollout if the canary is healthy — best when you want to validate a risky change against real traffic with minimal blast radius.

title: Stop leaking secrets in the CI pipeline
prompt: A CI pipeline hardcodes a cloud API key directly in the workflow YAML, echoes environment variables for debugging (printing the secret to public build logs), and bakes credentials into Docker image layers. Identify each leak and rewrite the pipeline to handle secrets safely, covering masked secret injection, avoiding image-layer leakage, and short-lived credentials via OIDC.
hints: Three leaks: the secret committed in YAML (in git history forever), the secret printed to logs by echoing env, and the secret baked into an image layer (extractable by anyone who pulls the image).
Never commit secrets. Inject them at runtime from the CI provider's encrypted secret store, which masks them in logs.
Don't echo env or print secrets; if you must pass a secret to a docker build, use BuildKit --secret mounts so it never lands in a layer, not --build-arg.
Best of all: eliminate long-lived keys. Use OIDC to let the CI job assume a cloud role for short-lived credentials, so there is no static secret to leak.
solutionCode:
# GitHub Actions: no hardcoded keys, OIDC for short-lived creds, masked secrets, no layer leak.
name: build-deploy
on: { push: { branches: [main] } }

permissions:
  id-token: write   # required for OIDC
  contents: read

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # No static AWS key anywhere. The job assumes a role via OIDC -> temporary credentials.
      - name: Configure AWS credentials (OIDC)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::111122223333:role/ci-deploy
          aws-region: eu-west-1
          # no aws-access-key-id / aws-secret-access-key fields at all

      # Secret injected from the encrypted store as an env var; Actions auto-masks it in logs.
      # We never 'echo' it.
      - name: Run migrations
        env:
          DB_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.DB_PASSWORD }}
        run: ./run-migrations.sh    # reads $DB_PASSWORD from env, never prints it

      # BuildKit secret mount: the secret is available DURING build but is NOT written
      # into any image layer (unlike --build-arg, which is recoverable from history).
      - name: Build image
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}" > npm_token.txt
          DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
            --secret id=npmtoken,src=npm_token.txt \
            -t registry/app:${{ github.sha }} .
          rm -f npm_token.txt
# Dockerfile uses:  RUN --mount=type=secret,id=npmtoken \
#                       npm ci --//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=$(cat /run/secrets/npmtoken)
# -> token is consumed at build time and leaves no trace in the final image.
explanation: There are three distinct leaks, each with its own fix. (1) Hardcoded key in YAML: a secret committed to the repository lives in git history permanently, visible to anyone with read access and impossible to fully scrub. The fix is to never commit secrets and instead inject them at runtime from the CI provider's encrypted secret store (here ${{ secrets.* }}), which also masks the value automatically in build logs. (2) Echoing env to logs: printing environment variables for debugging dumps the secret into build logs that, for public repos or shared CI, are widely readable — and CI log masking only catches known secret values, not arbitrary transformations. The fix is simply to never echo secrets; pass them to the program via env and let the program consume them without printing. (3) Credentials baked into image layers: using --build-arg or COPYing a credentials file writes the secret into an image layer, and because layers are content-addressed and distributed, anyone who pulls the image can extract it with docker history or by unpacking the layer — even if a later layer 'deletes' the file, the earlier layer still contains it. The fix is BuildKit secret mounts (--mount=type=secret): the secret is mounted into the build step's filesystem only for that RUN and is never persisted to any layer, so the final image is clean. The strongest control is eliminating long-lived secrets entirely: with OIDC, the CI job presents a signed identity token to the cloud provider and assumes an IAM role, receiving short-lived temporary credentials scoped to that job. There is no static access key stored anywhere to leak, the credentials expire in minutes, and access is tied to the specific repository and workflow via the role's trust policy. Together these move the pipeline from 'secrets everywhere, forever' to 'no static secrets, masked injection, and nothing recoverable from artifacts.'

title: Build an O(1) LRU cache
prompt: Implement a fixed-capacity Least Recently Used (LRU) cache supporting get(key) and put(key, value), both in O(1) average time. When the cache is full, putting a new key must evict the least recently used entry. Explain why the naive array/list approach is O(n) and which two data structures combine to achieve O(1).
hints: Tracking 'least recently used' needs an ordering by recency that you can update on every access. A plain array forces O(n) to find and move an element.
Use a hash map for O(1) key lookup, mapping each key to a node.
Use a doubly linked list to maintain recency order: most-recently-used at the front, least at the back. Moving a node to the front and removing the tail are O(1) with direct node pointers.
On get: look up the node, splice it out and re-insert at the front. On put past capacity: insert at front and evict the tail node (also delete its key from the map).
explanation: An LRU cache must do two things on every operation: find an entry by key, and update its recency so the least-recently-used entry can be evicted when capacity is exceeded. A naive approach that stores entries in an array or simple list ordered by recency makes lookup or the recency-move O(n): finding a key requires a linear scan, and moving an element to the 'most recent' position requires shifting elements. The O(1) solution combines two structures that each handle one concern. A hash map gives O(1) lookup from key to the entry's node. A doubly linked list maintains recency order explicitly: the node just after the head sentinel is the most recently used and the node just before the tail sentinel is the least recently used. Because the map hands us a direct pointer to a node, we can unlink it and re-insert it at the front in constant time using only neighbor-pointer updates — no scanning. Sentinel head and tail nodes remove all the null-checking edge cases at the list ends, keeping the splice operations branch-free. On get, we look the node up in the map (O(1)), then move it to the front to mark it most-recently-used (O(1)). On put, if the key exists we update and move it to the front; if it is new and the cache is at capacity, we read tail.prev (the least-recently-used node), unlink it, and delete its key from the map before inserting the new node at the front. Every path touches a constant number of pointers and one or two map operations, so both get and put run in O(1) average time, with O(capacity) space. The key insight is decomposition: the hash map solves lookup, the doubly linked list solves ordering, and the node pointers stored in the map let the two structures stay in sync in constant time.

title: Detect a cycle in a linked list
prompt: Given the head of a singly linked list, determine whether it contains a cycle, using O(1) extra space. A cycle exists if some node's next pointer points back to a previously visited node. Explain why the two-pointer (Floyd's) approach works and why it must terminate.
hints: A hash set of visited nodes detects a cycle but uses O(n) space. The constraint is O(1) space, so you need a pointer trick.
Move two pointers at different speeds through the list: a slow pointer one step at a time, a fast pointer two steps at a time.
If there is no cycle, fast reaches the end (null) and you return false.
If there is a cycle, fast eventually laps slow and they meet — because inside the loop the gap between them closes by one each step.
explanation: The straightforward solution records every visited node in a hash set and reports a cycle the first time it revisits a node, but that costs O(n) extra space, which the problem forbids. Floyd's Tortoise and Hare achieves O(1) space using two pointers moving at different speeds: slow advances one node per iteration, fast advances two. If the list has no cycle, fast (or fast.next) eventually reaches null at the end and the loop exits with false — this is also why the loop is guaranteed to terminate in the acyclic case. If the list does contain a cycle, both pointers eventually enter the loop, and once both are inside it, consider their relative position: each iteration fast moves two steps and slow moves one, so the distance from fast to slow decreases by exactly one step per iteration (modulo the loop length). A gap that strictly decreases by one each step cannot skip over zero, so it must eventually become zero — the pointers land on the same node and we return true. This guarantees termination in the cyclic case too: within at most O(loop length) iterations after both pointers are in the loop, they meet. Total time is O(n): the slow pointer travels at most the length of the list before a meeting occurs. The bonus function exploits a classic property — if you reset one pointer to the head after the meeting and then advance both pointers one step at a time, they meet exactly at the node where the cycle begins, which follows from the algebra of the distances (the distance from the head to the cycle entrance equals the distance from the meeting point to the entrance, going around). The core idea to remember is that differing speeds turn 'have I seen this node before?' into a constant-space convergence problem.

title: Turn an O(n^2) duplicate check into O(n)
prompt: A function checks whether an array contains any duplicate by comparing every pair of elements with nested loops, which is O(n^2) and times out on large inputs. Rewrite it to O(n) average time, and explain the time/space tradeoff and the underlying complexity analysis. Then state when the O(n^2) or a sort-based O(n log n) approach might still be preferable.
hints: Count the work in the nested loops: for each of n elements you compare against up to n others => about n^2/2 comparisons => O(n^2).
You can trade space for time: a hash set gives O(1) average membership tests.
Scan once; for each element, if it's already in the set you found a duplicate, otherwise add it. One pass = O(n) time.
The cost is O(n) extra space. If memory is tight or you can mutate the input, sorting then scanning neighbors is O(n log n) time and O(1) extra space.
explanation: The nested-loop version compares element i against every later element j, performing roughly n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 comparisons, which is Theta(n^2). Doubling the input quadruples the work, so it times out on large arrays. The linear solution trades space for time using a hash set, which supports membership tests and insertions in O(1) average time. We scan the array once: for each element we check whether it is already in the set (a seen duplicate) and otherwise add it. A single pass of n elements with O(1) work each gives O(n) average time, at the cost of O(n) extra space to hold the set. The 'average' caveat matters: hash operations are O(1) expected but degrade to O(n) worst case under pathological hash collisions, so the rigorous statement is O(n) average / O(n) space. When is something other than the hash approach preferable? If memory is severely constrained, the sort-based approach runs in O(n log n) time with only O(1) extra space (sorting in place), because after sorting any duplicates are adjacent and a single neighbor-comparison pass finds them. If the array is tiny, the O(n^2) version can actually be fastest in practice due to zero allocation and great cache locality — asymptotic complexity describes growth, not small-n constants, so for n in the low single digits the simple double loop wins. And if you also need to know which values are duplicated or their counts, a hash map (rather than a set) is the natural choice. The general lesson is the classic time-space tradeoff: you converted a quadratic-time, constant-space algorithm into a linear-time, linear-space one by spending memory to make lookups constant.
